metadata.title: Gender differences in lipid and glucose kinetics during short-term fasting
metadata.journal: American Journal of Physiology - Endocrinology and Metabolism
metadata.study_design: Experimental study with stable isotope labeled tracers measuring whole body glycerol and glucose kinetics at 14 and 22 hours of fasting
metadata.demographics: Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), matched on percent body fat (24±2% and 23±2% body weight as fat, respectively)

evidence.quote: Gender differences in lipid and glucose kinetics during short-term fasting
BETTINA MITTENDORFER, JEFFREY F. HOROWITZ, AND SAMUEL KLEIN
Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab 281: E1333–E1339, 2001
Six women and six men who were matched for adiposity (24±2 and 23±2% body wt as fat, respectively)
Substrate kinetics were measured by infusing stable isotope labeled tracers of glucose ([2H2]glucose) and glycerol ([2H5]glycerol)
We evaluated whole body lipid and glucose kinetics during basal (14-h fast) and short-term fasting (22-h fast) conditions
Six men (32 6 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 6 2 yr old), who were matched on percent body fat, participated in this study

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - health status
methods_participants.value: All subjects considered in good health after comprehensive medical evaluation including history, physical examination, electrocardiogram, and standard blood and urine tests

evidence.quote: All subjects were considered to be in good health after completing a comprehensive medical evaluation that included a history and physical examination, an electrocardiogram, and standard blood and urine tests

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criterion - Female menstrual cycle phase
methods_participants.value: None of the subjects was taking regular medications or smoked tobacco

evidence.quote: None of the subjects was taking regular medications or smoked tobacco
Each subject's fat mass (FM) and fat-free mass (FFM) were determined by dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (Hologic QDR 1000/w, Waltham, MA)
a constant infusion of [6,6-2H 2 ]glucose (priming dose: 18 mmol/kg

methods_participants.label: Statistical analysis method
methods_participants.value: Two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with gender and time as factors, followed by post hoc Student's t-test for multiple comparisons

evidence.quote: between 0930 and 1000 (for the 14 h of fasting)

methods_participants.label: Pre-study meal timing and composition
methods_participants.value: At 1800, standard meal containing 12 kcal/kg body weight (55% carbohydrate, 30% fat, 15% protein); At 2000, liquid formula (Ensure) containing 250 kcal (40 g carbohydrates, 6.1 g fat, 8.8 g protein)

evidence.quote: At 1800, subjects consumed a standard meal containing 12 kcal/kg body wt (55% of total energy as carbohydrate, 30% as fat, and 15% as protein). At 2000, the subjects ingested a liquid formula (Ensure; Ross Laboratories, Columbus, OH), containing 250 kcal (40 g carbohydrates, 6.1 g fat, and 8.8 g protein)
Plasma insulin (13) and glucagon (15) concentrations were measured by radioimmunoassay
A value of P #0.05 was considered to be statistically significant

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - body weight and exercise
methods_participants.value: All subjects had stable body weight for ≥2 months and had been sedentary (regular exercise <1 h/wk) for ≥6 months before the study

evidence.quote: All subjects had a stable body weight for $2 mo and had been sedentary (regular exercise ,1h/wk)for$6mobeforethestudy

narrative_results.statement: The relative increase in glycerol rate of appearance with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men

evidence.quote: However, the relative increase in glycerol R_a with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men (40±7 vs. 80±4% increase; P < 0.05)

narrative_results.statement: Sexual dimorphism exists in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions

evidence.quote: These data demonstrate the presence of sexual dimorphism in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Atwo-wayanalysisofvariance(ANOVA),withgenderand timeasfactors,wasusedtotestforsignificantdifferencesin substrate kinetics between men and women during fasting
ANOVA with gender and time as factors

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Gas chromatography with heptadecanoic acid internal standard
A two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), with gender and time as factors, was used to test for significant differences in substrate kinetics between men and women during fasting
Two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with gender and time as factors
Basal glycerol rate of appearance (R_a) in plasma, an indicator of whole body lipolytic rate, was greater in women than in men (2.1±0.2 vs. 1.5±0.1 mmol·kg body wt⁻¹·min⁻¹; P < 0.05)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Basal glycerol rate of appearance (R_a) in plasma, an indicator of whole body lipolytic rate, was greater in women than in men (2.1±0.2 vs. 1.5±0.1 mmol·kg body wt^-1·min^-1; P < 0.05)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: In contrast, glucose R_a was similar in men and women at 14 h (11±0.6 vs. 12±0.7 mmol·kg body wt⁻¹·min⁻¹) and 22 h of fasting (9±0.6 vs. 10±0.6 mmol·kg body wt⁻¹·min⁻¹)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: In contrast, glucose R_a was similar in men and women at 14 h (11±0.6 vs. 12±0.7 mmol·kg body wt^-1·min^-1) and 22 h of fasting (9±0.6 vs. 10±0.6 mmol·kg body wt^-1·min^-1)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma insulin concentration decreased more in women than in men with continued fasting, resulting in significantly lower plasma insulin in women at 22 hours of fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma insulin concentration was significantly lower in women than in men at 22 hours of fasting

semantic_groups.rationale: Pass 1 contains inclusion criteria about health status; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 describes medications/smoking criteria; Pass 2 describes menstrual cycle phase - completely different inclusion criteria
Both describe body weight stability requirement of ≥2 months; Pass 1 includes additional sedentary activity criterion not in Pass 2
Pass 1 contains menstrual cycle phase inclusion criterion; Pass 2 is empty
Both describe DXA method for body composition; Pass 2 adds specific equipment model (Hologic QDR 1000/w)
Both describe priming dose of 18 mmol/kg for [6,6-2H2]glucose isotope infusion; minor formatting differences
Both describe infusion rate of 0.22 mmol/kg body wt/min for [6,6-2H2]glucose; notation differences are equivalent
Both describe duration of 210 minutes for glucose isotope infusion; only unit notation differs (min vs minutes)
Both describe the same priming dose of 1.8 mmol/kg using [1,1,2,3,2H5]glycerol. Minor wording differences in phrasing ("Glycerol isotope infusion" vs "Glycerol infusion") are negligible.
Both describe the same infusion rate of 0.12 mmol/kg body weight/min using [1,1,2,3,2H5]glycerol. Unit notation differs slightly (·min⁻¹ vs /min) but represents identical values.
Both describe the same duration of 90 minutes for glycerol isotope infusion. Minor wording differences ("minutes" vs "min") are negligible.
Both describe the same initial fasting duration of 14 hours. Pass_2 adds context about isotope infusion study, but the core fact is identical.
Both describe the same extended fasting duration of 22 hours. Pass_2 adds context about isotope infusion study, but the core fact is identical.
Pass_1 contains meal composition data (12 kcal/kg with macronutrient breakdown) but Pass_2 is empty. This information is only in one extraction.
Pass_1 contains liquid formula composition data (250 kcal with specific macronutrient amounts) but Pass_2 is empty. This information is only in one extraction.
Pass_1 contains blood sampling timing information (every 10 minutes between 0930 and 1000) but Pass_2 is empty. This information is only in one extraction.
Pass 2 is empty; only Pass 1 contains information about blood sampling timing
Both describe plasma insulin assay by radioimmunoassay; Pass 1 adds 'RIA' abbreviation which is redundant
Both describe plasma glucagon assay by radioimmunoassay; Pass 1 adds 'RIA' abbreviation which is redundant
Both describe plasma catecholamine assay by single isotope derivative radioenzymatic method; Pass 1 adds 'Radioenzymatic assay' as alternative phrasing
Both describe plasma glucose assay using Yellow Springs Instrument analyzer; minor abbreviation difference ('analyzer' vs 'Instrument')
Both describe plasma glycerol assay by GC-MS; Pass 1 specifies internal standard [2-13C]glycerol while Pass 2 omits this detail
Both use gas chromatography but differ in internal standard specification and instrument model; Pass 1 mentions heptadecanoic acid standard, Pass 2 specifies Hewlett-Packard 5890-II
Both describe two-way ANOVA with gender and time as factors; wording order differs but content is identical
Both describe the same statistical significance threshold of P ≤ 0.05, with minor formatting differences
Pass 1 provides complete demographic data; Pass 2 extracts only the women count (6). Both are accurate but Pass 1 is more comprehensive
Pass 2 contains men's age data (32 ± 4 years) with no corresponding entry in Pass 1
Pass 2 contains women's age data (27 ± 2 years) with no corresponding entry in Pass 1
Pass 2 contains inclusion criterion for physical activity with no corresponding entry in Pass 1
Pass 2 contains assay methodology details with no corresponding entry in Pass 1
Pass 2 contains men participant count (6) with no corresponding entry in Pass 1
Pass 2 adds '; gender difference' annotation which is implicit in the statement but doesn't change the core fact
Identical statements across both passes
Identical statements across both passes
Pass 1 provides more detail about the temporal pattern (decreased more with continued fasting), while Pass 2 focuses on the endpoint result. Both convey the same core finding with different emphasis
Pass 2 adds '; gender difference' annotation which is implicit in the statement but doesn't change the core fact
Pass 1 contains a complete statement while Pass 2 is empty; orphaned extraction from Pass 1
Pass 2 adds '; gender difference' annotation which is implicit in the statement but doesn't change the core fact
